title: Financial ratios
summary: Turn the income statement and balance sheet into comparable signals about liquidity, activity, leverage, profitability, and integrated reading.

topics.title: Liquidity
topics.summary: Measures the ability to meet short-term obligations without putting unnecessary pressure on operations.

sections.title: Core idea
sections.body: Liquidity ratios answer one simple question: can the company convert current assets into enough cash to pay what is due soon? Asset quality matters as much as size.
bullets: The current ratio compares all current assets against current liabilities.
The quick ratio removes inventory to create a more conservative view.
The cash ratio looks only at the most immediate coverage: cash and equivalents.

formulaLegend.meaning: Cash, net receivables, advances, and inventory expected to turn within the operating cycle.
Obligations due within the operating cycle or the next year.
The least liquid part of current assets because it must be sold before becoming cash.

sections.title: When to use it and common mistakes
sections.body: These ratios are useful when reviewing short-term cash pressure, credit lines, or whether the business can absorb collection delays.
bullets: A low value usually signals working-capital stress or refinancing dependence.
An excessively high value may also suggest idle resources.
Do not confuse good liquidity with high profitability.

exampleTable.title: Mini guided case

cells: Without selling inventory, the company looks tighter.
Immediate cash backing is thin; collections matter.

exampleTable.footnote: Final interpretation should always be compared with the operating cycle.

topics.title: Activity
topics.summary: Links sales and cost of sales with inventory, receivables, payables, and assets to measure speed and efficiency.

sections.title: Turnover and average days
sections.body: Activity ratios answer how fast working capital moves. A company can sell a lot and still manage inventory poorly or collect too slowly.
bullets: High turnover usually means more efficient use of the resource being measured.
Average periods convert the ratio into days, which is often easier to teach and interpret.
The value comes from reading inventory, collection, and payment together.

formulaLegend.meaning: Cost of the goods actually sold during the period.
Accounts receivable minus allowance for doubtful accounts.
Annual convention used to convert a turnover ratio into average days.

sections.title: What to read together
sections.body: This block should rarely be read one ratio at a time. The real insight comes from connecting inventory, collection, and payment.
bullets: If you collect in 75 days but pay suppliers in 30, the cash cycle is under pressure.
Low total asset turnover can come from weak sales or from an oversized asset base.
Fixed asset turnover helps assess whether installed capacity is underused.

cells: Product is moving slowly or purchases are too high.
Loose credit policy or slow collections.
The company uses little spontaneous supplier financing.

topics.title: Debt and coverage
topics.summary: Evaluates how much of the financial structure depends on creditors and how comfortably earnings cover interest expense.

sections.title: Exposure and support
sections.body: Debt ratios show structural leverage. Interest coverage adds an operating question: does the business generate enough earnings to carry its debt?
bullets: Debt ratio compares total liabilities against total assets.
Debt-to-equity shows creditor pressure relative to owners' capital.
Interest coverage connects EBIT with financing cost.

formulaLegend.meaning: The sum of current and non-current obligations.
Capital and retained earnings attributable to shareholders.
Operating earnings before interest and taxes.

sections.title: Prudent reading
sections.body: More debt is not automatically bad, but it does require a careful review of sales stability, coverage, and cash flexibility.
bullets: A high equity multiplier can boost ROE, but it also raises risk.
Interest coverage near 1x leaves almost no cushion.
Reading debt and coverage together avoids a one-angle conclusion.

cells: Any operating decline creates immediate pressure.

topics.title: Profitability
topics.summary: Measures the ability to turn sales and assets into earnings for the operation and for shareholders.

sections.title: Margins and returns
sections.body: Profitability ratios connect earnings quality with sales volume and asset size.
bullets: Gross margin reflects price, mix, and cost of sales.
Operating margin shows what remains after selling and administrative expenses.
ROA and ROE connect net income with assets and equity.

formulaLegend.meaning: Net sales minus cost of sales.
Final profit after interest and taxes.
Returns that connect earnings with invested resources.

sections.title: Common interpretation mistakes
sections.body: One margin alone is never enough. A company can have a modest net margin and still produce a strong ROE if it moves assets quickly and uses debt carefully.
bullets: A healthy gross margin does not guarantee net income if operating expenses grow too fast.
Low ROA with high turnover can signal very thin margins.
High ROE without reviewing debt can lead to an overly optimistic conclusion.

cells: The cost structure is becoming more efficient.
The company is using more assets without enough profit.
Leverage may be driving shareholder return.

topics.title: Integrated analysis / DuPont
topics.summary: Breaks ROE into net margin, asset turnover, and equity multiplier to explain where shareholder return is coming from.

sections.title: How to read DuPont
sections.body: DuPont keeps you from treating ROE as a single number. It separates whether the return comes from better profit on sales, better use of assets, or more financial leverage.

formulaLegend.meaning: How much of each sale becomes net income.
How many sales are generated by the asset base.
How much asset base is supported by each unit of equity.

sections.title: Mini guided case and common mistakes
sections.body: If ROE improves, DuPont helps identify the source. That prevents celebrating an improvement that comes only from higher leverage.
bullets: If net margin rises, the gain came from better final profitability.
If turnover rises, the company is selling more per unit of assets.
If the multiplier rises, ROE may be increasing only because of more leverage.

cells: Is the company converting sales into profit more effectively?
Are assets generating enough sales volume?
Is ROE depending too much on leverage?

calculator.title: Financial ratios accounting builder
calculator.summary: Build a summarized income statement and balance sheet. The system derives subtotals, validates the accounting equation, and then groups the ratios by family.
calculator.note: Only capture the base line items. The builder calculates subtotals automatically.

steps.title: Step 1 · Income statement
steps.summary: Capture sales, inventories, purchases, and expenses to derive net sales, cost of sales, EBIT, and net income.

steps.title: Step 2 · Balance sheet
steps.summary: Capture assets, liabilities, and equity. The builder validates that assets = liabilities + equity.

steps.title: Step 3 · Results and interpretation
steps.summary: Review ratios by family, key formulas, and didactic reading for each indicator.

sections.title: Sales and cost base
sections.summary: These line items feed net sales and cost of sales.

fields.label: Gross sales
fields.helperText: Total billed revenue before discounts and returns.

fields.label: Sales discounts
fields.helperText: Allowances or rebates granted to customers.

fields.label: Beginning inventory
fields.helperText: Inventory at the start of the period.

fields.label: Ending inventory
fields.helperText: Inventory at the end of the period. It must match the inventory reported on the balance sheet.

sections.title: Expenses and final result
sections.summary: These line items complete the path from gross profit to net income.

fields.label: Selling expenses
fields.helperText: Commercial salaries, commissions, freight, and other selling costs.

fields.label: Income tax expense
fields.helperText: Tax accrued on the period's earnings.

sections.title: Current and non-current assets
sections.summary: Capture liquid assets, receivables, inventory, and productive assets.

fields.label: Cash and equivalents
fields.helperText: Immediate available cash and cash equivalents.

fields.label: Allowance for doubtful accounts
fields.helperText: Adjustment used to report receivables at net value.

fields.label: Inventory
fields.helperText: Balance sheet inventory. It must equal ending inventory from the income statement.

fields.label: Accumulated depreciation
fields.helperText: Accumulated adjustment to bring fixed assets to net value.

sections.title: Liabilities and equity
sections.summary: Complete the financing structure and validate the accounting equation.

fields.label: Short-term bank debt
fields.helperText: Loans and overdrafts due in the short term.

fields.label: Equity
fields.helperText: Capital and retained earnings attributable to owners.

results.label: Current ratio
results.summary: Overall short-term coverage of obligations.

didacticRanges.label: Tight
didacticRanges.summary: Short-term coverage looks weak; review working capital pressure.

didacticRanges.label: Reasonable
didacticRanges.summary: There is acceptable short-term breathing room.

didacticRanges.label: Loose
didacticRanges.summary: There is a strong liquidity cushion; check for idle resources.

results.label: Quick ratio
results.summary: Short-term liquidity without relying on inventory sales.

didacticRanges.label: Weak
didacticRanges.summary: Without inventory, immediate support looks thin.

didacticRanges.label: Adequate
didacticRanges.summary: The company keeps reasonable coverage even without inventory.

results.label: Cash ratio
results.summary: Immediate coverage using available cash only.

didacticRanges.label: Very narrow
didacticRanges.summary: Immediate cash is tight and depends heavily on operating inflows.

didacticRanges.label: Moderate
didacticRanges.summary: There is some immediate backing, though not abundant.

didacticRanges.label: Strong
didacticRanges.summary: Immediate cash comfortably covers short-term pressure.

results.label: Net working capital
results.summary: Monetary short-term surplus after covering current obligations.

results.label: Inventory turnover
results.summary: Frequency with which inventory is converted into cost of sales.

results.label: Days in inventory
results.summary: Average time inventory remains in stock.

results.label: Receivables turnover
results.summary: Speed at which credit sales are collected.

results.label: Average collection period
results.summary: Average number of days to collect from customers.

results.label: Payables turnover
results.summary: Speed of trade debt rotation with suppliers.

results.label: Average payment period
results.summary: Average number of days the company takes to pay suppliers.

results.label: Total asset turnover
results.summary: Use of the total asset base to generate sales.

results.label: Fixed asset turnover
results.summary: Use of installed capacity and productive assets.

results.label: Debt ratio
results.summary: Share of assets financed by creditors.

results.label: Debt to equity
results.summary: Creditor pressure relative to owners' capital.

results.label: Equity multiplier
results.summary: Degree to which equity supports the asset base through leverage.

results.label: Interest coverage
results.summary: Number of times operating earnings cover interest cost.

results.label: Gross margin
results.summary: Share of each sale that remains after cost of sales.

results.label: Operating margin
results.summary: Share of each sale left after operating expenses.

results.label: Net margin
results.summary: Final share of sales that becomes net income.

results.label: ROA
results.summary: Return generated by the total asset base.

results.label: ROE
results.summary: Return generated for shareholders.

results.label: Component 1 · Net margin
results.summary: First ROE driver in DuPont analysis.

results.label: Component 2 · Asset turnover
results.summary: Second ROE driver: asset efficiency.

results.label: Component 3 · Equity multiplier
results.summary: Third ROE driver: financial leverage intensity.

results.label: ROE by DuPont
results.formula: Net margin × Asset turnover × Equity multiplier
results.summary: Integrated reconstruction of shareholder return.
